clean_story:
Stolen treasures. Empty display cases.

The term museum ethics debates refers to arguments over who rightfully owns artifacts—like a schoolyard fight over a borrowed toy, but with 3,000-year-old statues.

Museums often hold objects taken during wars or colonial rule. The Parthenon Marbles, carved for Athens’ Acropolis, were removed by a British lord in 1801 with questionable permission. Greece calls it theft; the British Museum calls it legal salvage. Similarly, Benin Bronzes—thousands of brass plaques looted from Nigeria in 1897—sit in European museums while Nigeria builds a new museum to house them, empty-handed.

Some argue museums preserve history; others say they erase it by keeping artifacts far from their cultural homes. France returned 26 looted artifacts to Benin in 2021, but 90,000 African objects remain in French museums alone.

Three related subjects are repatriation laws, colonial-era looting routes, and digital copies vs originals.
